_purpose: Courthouse Atlas for Lunenburg County, VA. Record survival, courthouse history, and known losses. Formed 1746 from Brunswick — the 'mother county' of Southside Virginia, from which Halifax, Charlotte, Mecklenburg, and parts of several other counties were carved. Lunenburg's records are the starting point for virtually all Southside genealogy.

current_courthouse.location: Lunenburg, VA — courthouse square on VA Route 40 (historic brick structure, one of the oldest continuously used courthouses in Virginia)

previous_courthouses.location: Lunenburg — original courthouse site near the present location
previous_courthouses.fate: Early wooden courthouse replaced as county government matured. Records transferred to successor buildings.
Replaced by the current 1827 brick courthouse. Records transferred intact.

record_losses.cause: None — no major courthouse fire or destruction
record_losses.description: Lunenburg County's courthouse has never experienced a catastrophic fire or destruction. Records survive continuously from the county's 1746 formation, making Lunenburg one of the most complete record repositories in Southside Virginia. This is genealogically critical because Lunenburg is the 'mother county' — the parent of Halifax (1752), Charlotte (1764), and Mecklenburg (1765), among others. Researchers tracing families in any of these daughter counties must eventually come to Lunenburg for the founding generation. The county's tithable lists, published by Landon C. Bell in Sunlight on the Southside, are among the most important genealogical compilations for the Southside.
record_losses.narrative_impact: {ancestor_name}'s earliest Southside records are almost certainly in Lunenburg County — the mother county from which every other Southside county was carved. If the family was here before 1752, Lunenburg is where the paper trail begins, and because no fire has ever destroyed these records, the trail is likely unbroken.
record_losses.source: Library of Virginia. A Guide to Virginia County and City Records on Microfilm. Richmond, 2001.

deeds.completeness: Excellent — continuous series from county formation. Lunenburg's deed books are the foundational land records for the entire Southside, covering territory that would later become multiple counties.
deeds.gaps: No significant gaps. Pre-1746 records for the same territory are in Brunswick County.
deeds.repository: Lunenburg County Clerk of Court, Lunenburg; Library of Virginia, Richmond
deeds.online_access: FamilySearch microfilm; Ancestry.com VA land records; Library of Virginia digital collections

wills_estates.completeness: Excellent — will books and estate records survive from county formation. The earliest wills document the founding generation of the Southside planter class.
wills_estates.gaps: No significant gaps in bound volumes. Loose estate papers from the earliest decades have some wear damage.
wills_estates.repository: Lunenburg County Clerk of Court; Library of Virginia
wills_estates.online_access: FamilySearch microfilm; Library of Virginia digital collections

marriages.completeness: Good — marriage bonds survive from county formation, an unusually early start for Virginia Southside counties.
marriages.gaps: Some bonds from the earliest years may be incomplete. Anglican church records supplement.
marriages.repository: Lunenburg County Clerk of Court; Library of Virginia

court_minutes.completeness: Excellent — continuous from formation. The court order books document slave patrols, road assignments, orphan bindings, and the full machinery of colonial and antebellum county government.
court_minutes.repository: Lunenburg County Clerk of Court; Library of Virginia

vital_records.completeness: Virginia state registration began 1912. Earlier vital events from church records and county court orders.
vital_records.repository: Virginia Department of Health, Office of Vital Records; Library of Virginia for delayed certificates

church_records.completeness: Cumberland Parish vestry records survive from the county's formation. The parish originally covered the vast territory that would become multiple counties. Tithable lists from the vestry books have been published and are essential population records. Baptist and Presbyterian records become significant after the 1760s.
church_records.repository: Library of Virginia; published in Bell, Sunlight on the Southside; individual church archives
church_records.online_access: Published transcriptions widely available; Library of Virginia microfilm

substitute_sources.description: Lunenburg was formed from Brunswick in 1746. All records for the Lunenburg territory before 1746 — deeds, wills, court orders — are filed under Brunswick County.
substitute_sources.repository: Brunswick County Clerk of Court, Lawrenceville; Library of Virginia
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Essential for any family in the Southside before 1746. Brunswick is the grandmother county — Lunenburg's parent, and the oldest organized government in the Southside.
substitute_sources.source: Library of Virginia. A Guide to Virginia County and City Records on Microfilm. Richmond, 2001.

substitute_sources.source_type: Landon C. Bell, Sunlight on the Southside
substitute_sources.description: Bell's published compilation of Lunenburg County tithable lists (1748-1783) is the single most important genealogical reference for the Southside. It provides annual lists of taxable white males and enslaved individuals by slaveholder, covering the period when Lunenburg was the mother county of the region.
substitute_sources.repository: Published volume widely available in genealogical libraries and used book markets
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Indispensable. The tithable lists function as annual census records and are the foundation for nearly every Southside family reconstruction. They document slaveholding patterns, population movement, and the appearance and disappearance of families from the tax rolls.
substitute_sources.source: Bell, Landon C. Sunlight on the Southside: Lists of Tithes, Lunenburg County, Virginia, 1748-1783. Philadelphia: George S. Ferguson Company, 1931.

substitute_sources.description: Population schedules 1790-1940. Lunenburg County enumerated from the first federal census. By 1790, the county had already given up territory to four daughter counties and was much smaller than its original extent.
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Essential baseline. The 1790 census shows the families that remained in the residual Lunenburg territory after the daughter counties were carved away.
substitute_sources.source: National Archives and Records Administration. Federal Census Schedules, Lunenburg County, VA.

substitute_sources.description: Colonial land patents for the Lunenburg territory document the initial wave of settlement moving west and south from the Tidewater in the 1740s and 1750s.
substitute_sources.repository: Library of Virginia, Richmond; published abstracts in Nugent, Cavaliers and Pioneers
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Patents document the earliest landholders and the headright system that drove settlement. For families arriving before 1746, patents may be the only documentation.
substitute_sources.source: Nugent, Nell Marion. Cavaliers and Pioneers: Abstracts of Virginia Land Patents and Grants, vol. 3. Richmond: Virginia State Library, 1979.

narrative_hooks: Lunenburg is the mother county of Southside Virginia — from its territory came Halifax, Charlotte, Mecklenburg, and pieces of others — and {ancestor_name}'s earliest Southside records are almost certainly filed here, in a courthouse that has never burned.

Bell's Sunlight on the Southside published Lunenburg's tithable lists from 1748 to 1783, and if {ancestor_name} was taxable in the Southside during those years, {pronoun_possessive} name appears in that book — an annual census before the census existed.

Lunenburg's records are intact from 1746, which makes it a genealogical anchor for the entire Southside — while daughter counties like Mecklenburg lost records to fire, the mother county's paper trail runs unbroken from the day the first clerk opened the first book.

Before 1746, Lunenburg was Brunswick County territory, and before 1720, it was Surry and Prince George — tracking {ancestor_name}'s family to the Southside's origins means following the records backward through three county formations to the Tidewater.

The Cumberland Parish vestry was Lunenburg's other government — its tithable lists documented every taxable person, and its processioning records walked the property lines that the deed books described.
source_references: Library of Virginia. A Guide to Virginia County and City Records on Microfilm. Richmond, 2001.
Bell, Landon C. Sunlight on the Southside: Lists of Tithes, Lunenburg County, Virginia, 1748-1783. Philadelphia: George S. Ferguson Company, 1931.
Nugent, Nell Marion. Cavaliers and Pioneers: Abstracts of Virginia Land Patents and Grants, vol. 3. Richmond: Virginia State Library, 1979.
Works Progress Administration. Historical Records Survey: Virginia County Records. Richmond, 1937-1942.
